claims.claim_text: Falco is a cloud-native runtime security tool that detects threats in running containers by monitoring system calls.
claims.rationale: The Falco repository README directly describes Falco as a cloud-native runtime security tool that observes syscall events and detects threats in real time.

claims.claim_text: Falco can detect shells in containers, suspicious sensitive-file reads, and cryptominer-like behavior.
claims.rationale: Falco's official default-rules documentation is the right primary source for these concrete detection examples, but `falco.org` is not on the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Falco integrates with Kubernetes admission controllers for runtime-informed deployment decisions.
claims.anchor_text: - **Integrate Falco with Kubernetes admission controllers for runtime-informed deployment decisions**
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Integrate Falco with Kubernetes audit signals and automated remediation workflows informed by runtime alerts**
claims.rationale: The module does not present a citable direct admission-controller integration path for this runtime claim, so the wording should be narrowed to workflows Falco actually documents.

claims.claim_text: Falco was created by Sysdig and donated to CNCF in 2018.
claims.anchor_text: Falco was created by Sysdig and donated to CNCF in 2018.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Falco was created and open sourced by Sysdig in 2016 and entered the CNCF Sandbox in October 2018.
claims.rationale: Current CNCF sources support Sysdig provenance and 2018 CNCF acceptance, but not the module's exact 'donated to CNCF in 2018' wording.

claims.claim_text: Falco uses eBPF to hook into the Linux kernel and monitor every system call with near-zero overhead.
claims.anchor_text: It uses eBPF (extended Berkeley Packet Filter) to hook into the Linux kernel and monitor every system call with near-zero overhead.
claims.suggested_rewrite: It uses kernel-level instrumentation such as eBPF probes to monitor system call events from running workloads.
claims.rationale: Allowlisted sources support kernel-level syscall monitoring via eBPF or kernel-module drivers, but not the stronger 'every system call' and 'near-zero overhead' wording.

claims.claim_text: Falco's legacy eBPF driver generally needs kernel 4.14+ and is categorically safer than the kernel module.
claims.anchor_text: | **eBPF** | No kernel module, safer, modern | Needs kernel 4.14+ |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **eBPF** | No kernel module, modern | Kernel support varies by architecture; check Falco's driver matrix |
claims.rationale: Falco's driver matrix is architecture-specific and the allowlisted sources do not cleanly support the blanket 'safer' claim.

claims.claim_text: Falco's modern eBPF (CO-RE) driver requires kernel 5.8+.
claims.rationale: The Falco libs repository publishes the official driver support matrix and lists modern eBPF support beginning at kernel 5.8 on supported architectures.

claims.claim_text: Falco rules use MITRE ATT&CK tags to map detections to attacker tactics.
claims.rationale: Falco's rule documentation is the most direct primary source for rule tags such as MITRE ATT&CK mappings, but it lives on off-allowlist `falco.org`.

claims.claim_text: The MITRE ATT&CK framework organizes adversary behavior into tactics such as Initial Access, Execution, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Defense Evasion, Credential Access, Discovery, Lateral Movement, Collection, Command and Control, Exfiltration, and Impact.
claims.rationale: MITRE ATT&CK's enterprise tactics reference directly enumerates these tactic categories.

claims.claim_text: Falcosidekick can trigger AWS Lambda functions on alerts.
claims.rationale: The Falcosidekick repository explicitly lists AWS Lambda as a supported serverless output.

claims.claim_text: A specific team built a Lambda that automatically captured memory dumps of suspicious containers before termination.
claims.anchor_text: One team built a Lambda that automatically captures memory dumps of suspicious containers before terminating them.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams can use alert-driven automation to trigger forensic capture or remediation steps before terminating suspicious workloads.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdote presented as a real case, so the named specificity must be collapsed into a general operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: Falco exposes Kubernetes and container metadata fields such as `k8s.pod.name`, `k8s.ns.name`, `k8s.deployment.name`, `container.image.repository`, and `container.image.tag`.
claims.rationale: The official Falco container plugin README documents these supported metadata fields directly.

claims.claim_text: Falco can detect threats across 350+ system calls.
claims.anchor_text: Falco can detect threats across 350+ system calls, but you don't need to know them all.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Falco can detect threats across many Linux system calls, but you don't need to know them all.
claims.rationale: The precise number is not backed by an allowlisted primary source here, so the statistic should be removed while preserving the lesson.

claims.claim_text: Falco community rules were updated within hours of Log4Shell to detect exploitation attempts, faster than most signature-based tools.
claims.anchor_text: When the Log4Shell vulnerability hit, <!-- incident-xref: log4shell --> Falco community rules were updated within hours to detect exploitation attempts—faster than most signature-based tools.
claims.suggested_rewrite: During major vulnerability outbreaks such as Log4Shell, <!-- incident-xref: log4shell --> the Falco community can add or update detection rules for related exploitation patterns.
claims.rationale: The exact timing and comparative performance claim are not adequately backed by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: A team ran Falco yet missed a cryptominer for 3 weeks because CPU growth was misread as normal traffic growth.
claims.anchor_text: *A team had Falco running but missed a cryptominer for 3 weeks. CPU usage was high but attributed to "traffic growth."*
claims.suggested_rewrite: *Teams can miss cryptomining activity when alert rules focus too narrowly on known indicators and rising CPU gets misattributed to normal workload changes.*
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with precise duration and diagnosis, so the false specifics must be removed.

claims.claim_text: The attacker disguised the miner under the process name `nginx-worker` instead of `xmrig`.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. Use a benign-looking process name instead of a well-known miner binary name
claims.rationale: The module presents a concrete attacker tactic without attribution, so the specific fake detail should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: The attacker hid mining-pool traffic inside HTTPS instead of `stratum+tcp`.
claims.anchor_text: 2. Connect to mining pool via HTTPS (not `stratum+tcp`)
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. Hide mining traffic inside common outbound protocols instead of obvious mining-specific ones
claims.rationale: This is another unattributed attack-detail claim that should keep the lesson while dropping the made-to-sound-real specifics.

claims.claim_text: The attacker throttled the miner to 50% CPU instead of 100%.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. Throttle CPU usage to stay below obvious saturation thresholds
claims.rationale: The exact percentages are unsupported false precision inside an unattributed anecdote.

further_reading.title: Falco Core Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream overview of Falco's runtime-security model, syscall monitoring, and project structure.

further_reading.title: Falco Helm Chart README
further_reading.why_relevant: Best allowlisted source for current installation patterns, driver selection, custom rules, and Falcosidekick integration.

further_reading.title: Falcosidekick Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Documents supported outputs and routing targets for sending Falco alerts into response and observability systems.

notes: The shared section pool has no Falco-specific sources. Several legitimate Falco-rule and Falco-doc claims land in `needs_allowlist_expansion` because the strongest primary sources live on `falco.org`, which is currently off-allowlist. Also note that current Falco chart docs deprecate legacy `driver.kind=ebpf` in favor of `modern_ebpf`, but code blocks were excluded from this seed.
